known_vulnerabilities.name: Double-Payout Vulnerability
known_vulnerabilities.description: In a naive implementation, if submit_result and finalize_match were separate calls, an oracle could call submit_result (storing a result), and then any user could call finalize_match to execute payout. This creates a window where the payout could theoretically happen twice if the same result is stored and executed in two separate transactions.
known_vulnerabilities.attack_scenario: 1. Oracle calls submit_result(match_id, winner) storing result and setting state=PendingResult; 2. Oracle or any actor calls submit_result again (or similar); 3. Payout executes multiple times
known_vulnerabilities.root_cause: Payout logic separated from result storage
mitigation_implemented: Payout execution is ATOMIC with state transitions
submit_result with dispute_period=0 immediately executes payout AND sets state=Completed
finalize_match can only execute when state=PendingResult AND dispute deadline passed
Once Completed (terminal), no further transitions trigger payouts
claim_vested_payout only sets a flag; cannot re-trigger payout
known_vulnerabilities.verification: INV-1 (No Double Payout), INV-8 (Escrow Balance Conservation)

known_vulnerabilities.name: Missing Refund in Cancellation
known_vulnerabilities.description: If cancel_match or expire_match fail to refund deposits, funds become trapped in the contract forever.
known_vulnerabilities.attack_scenario: 1. Both players deposit stakes; 2. Match is cancelled; 3. Refund logic fails silently; 4. Funds remain in contract
known_vulnerabilities.root_cause: Incomplete refund paths in cancel/expire logic
mitigation_implemented: cancel_match: if player1_deposited, transfer stake to player1; if player2_deposited, transfer stake to player2
expire_match: identical refund logic
Both paths use token::Client::transfer which will panic if transfer fails
All refund paths cover both player1_deposited and player2_deposited flags
known_vulnerabilities.verification: INV-2 (No Fund Loss), INV-8 (Escrow Balance Conservation)

known_vulnerabilities.name: Unreachable Funds (Dead States)
known_vulnerabilities.description: If a match gets stuck in a non-terminal state with escrowed funds and no legal transition out, those funds become inaccessible.
known_vulnerabilities.attack_scenario: 1. Match in Active state with full escrow; 2. Oracle becomes unavailable; 3. submit_result never called; 4. No timeout mechanism for Active state; 5. Funds trapped indefinitely
known_vulnerabilities.root_cause: Incomplete state transition paths; missing escape hatches
mitigation_implemented: Pending state: can timeout via expire_match after MatchTimeout ledgers
Active state: can submit_result (immediate or delayed payout)
PendingResult state: has ResultDeadline for finalize_match
Paused state: can resume_match or timeout as Pending
Admin can set dispute_period=0 for immediate payouts (no PendingResult delay)
All non-terminal states have at least one legal transition
known_vulnerabilities.residual_risk: MEDIUM - Active state lacks direct timeout; relies on oracle
known_vulnerabilities.remediation: Consider adding timeout for Active state transitions (optional future enhancement)

known_vulnerabilities.name: Replay Attack on Payout
known_vulnerabilities.description: If submit_result could be called multiple times without state changes, an oracle could replay payout multiple times.
known_vulnerabilities.attack_scenario: 1. Oracle calls submit_result(match_id, winner); 2. State changes to PendingResult or Completed; 3. Oracle calls submit_result again with same params; 4. State guards prevent re-execution
mitigation_implemented: submit_result requires Match.state == Active
After first call, state becomes PendingResult or Completed
Second call with same match_id fails: InvalidState (not Active anymore)
Prevents replay within same block or across blocks

known_vulnerabilities.name: Duplicate Deposit
known_vulnerabilities.description: A player could attempt to deposit twice, inflating their escrow or creating accounting mismatches.
known_vulnerabilities.attack_scenario: 1. player1 calls deposit(match_id); 2. Match transitions to Active; 3. player1 calls deposit again; 4. Duplicate funds transferred?
mitigation_implemented: deposit checks: is_p1 && m.player1_deposited ? Err(AlreadyFunded) : continue
deposit checks: is_p2 && m.player2_deposited ? Err(AlreadyFunded) : continue
Flags prevent any re-deposit after first deposit

known_vulnerabilities.name: Unauthorized State Mutation
known_vulnerabilities.description: If authorization checks were missing, any address could create matches, submit results, or transfer admin.
known_vulnerabilities.attack_scenario: 1. Attacker calls create_match without authorization; 2. Attacker calls submit_result as fake oracle; 3. Attacker transfers admin to themselves
mitigation_implemented: create_match requires player1.require_auth()
deposit requires player.require_auth()
submit_result requires oracle.require_auth()
pause_match / resume_match require caller.require_auth()
All admin functions require admin.require_auth()
Soroban SDK enforces signature verification for each require_auth()

known_vulnerabilities.name: Ambiguous Winner Logic
known_vulnerabilities.description: If winner determination logic is ambiguous, incorrect payouts could occur (wrong winner receiving funds).
known_vulnerabilities.attack_scenario: 1. Oracle submits winner=Player1; 2. Payout logic incorrectly transfers to Player2; 3. Wrong player enriched
mitigation_implemented: Winner is exhaustive Rust enum: Player1, Player2, Draw
execute_payout uses match expression (compiler enforces exhaustiveness)
Player1 -> transfer full pot to player1
Player2 -> transfer full pot to player2
Draw -> transfer stake to player1 AND stake to player2
No ambiguity; no missing cases

known_vulnerabilities.name: Partial Payout (Fund Leakage)
known_vulnerabilities.description: If payout logic transfers less than the full escrowed amount, remaining funds leak into contract reserves.
known_vulnerabilities.attack_scenario: 1. Pot = 2x stake; 2. Payout logic transfers 1.5x stake to winner; 3. 0.5x stake orphaned in contract
mitigation_implemented: All paths calculate: 2 * stake_amount for non-draw winners
Draw refunds stake to each player (total 2x)
No rounding or partial transfers
execute_payout is deterministic based on stake_amount and Winner variant

known_vulnerabilities.name: Oracle Result Tampering Post-Dispute
known_vulnerabilities.description: If dispute voting could change the original oracle result mid-vote, integrity is compromised.
known_vulnerabilities.attack_scenario: 1. Oracle submits winner=Player1; 2. Dispute raised; 3. During voting, result changes to winner=Player2; 4. Vote on outdated result
mitigation_implemented: Match.winner is immutable once set by submit_result
PendingWinner stored separately as dispute record
resolve_dispute_by_vote reads PendingWinner (original result)
Dispute can only uphold or overturn (=Draw); cannot change to different winner
All winner changes are deterministic and audit-able

known_vulnerabilities.name: Premature Dispute Finalization
known_vulnerabilities.description: If dispute deadline enforcement is weak, match could be finalized before all players have a chance to dispute.
known_vulnerabilities.attack_scenario: 1. Oracle submits result (dispute_period = 100 ledgers); 2. finalize_match called at ledger 50; 3. Result finalized before deadline; 4. Players unable to dispute
mitigation_implemented: submit_result sets ResultDeadline = current_ledger + dispute_period
finalize_match checks: current_ledger >= deadline ? ok : Err(DisputePeriodNotElapsed)
Explicit ledger comparison blocks early finalization
Deadline stored persistently; cannot be altered post-submission

known_vulnerabilities.name: Vote Splitting / Double Voting
known_vulnerabilities.description: If vote tracking is weak, a voter could vote multiple times, skewing dispute outcomes.
known_vulnerabilities.attack_scenario: 1. Voter A calls vote_on_dispute(dispute_id, true); 2. Voter A calls vote_on_dispute(dispute_id, false); 3. Vote recorded twice with opposite intent
mitigation_implemented: vote_on_dispute stores DisputeVote(dispute_id, voter) = vote_value
Check: has(&DisputeVote(dispute_id, voter)) ? Err(AlreadyVoted) : continue
Composite key includes both dispute_id and voter address
Prevents any duplicate voting on same dispute

known_vulnerabilities.name: Tier Bypass / Stake Limit Evasion
known_vulnerabilities.description: If tier validation is incomplete, low-tier players could create/join high-stake matches, increasing exposure.
known_vulnerabilities.attack_scenario: 1. Bronze player (1-100 range) calls create_match(stake=500); 2. Tier check missed; 3. Player exposed to unsustainable risk
known_vulnerabilities.root_cause: Missing tier validation on creation or deposit
mitigation_implemented: create_match calls: require_player_tier_for_stake(player1, stake_amount)
create_match calls: require_player_tier_for_stake(player2, stake_amount)
deposit calls: require_player_tier_for_stake(player, m.stake_amount)
Tier determined by completed_match_count with thresholds: Bronze 0, Silver 3, Gold 6, Platinum 10
known_vulnerabilities.residual_risk: LOW - Tier validated on creation and deposit

known_vulnerabilities.name: Unvetted Token Acceptance
known_vulnerabilities.description: If token allowlist is not enforced, malicious or fake tokens could be used in matches, leading to failed transfers or rug-pulls.
known_vulnerabilities.attack_scenario: 1. Allowlist disabled or not checked; 2. Attacker creates match with fake token; 3. Player deposits fake tokens; 4. Token transfer fails silently or contract drained
mitigation_implemented: create_match checks: if allowlist_enforced && !is_token_allowed(token) -> Err(TokenNotAllowed)
AllowlistEnforced is true once first token is added via add_allowed_token
Admin can manage allowlist before allowing matches
Deposit fails if token isn't callable (token::Client::transfer panics on invalid token)

known_vulnerabilities.name: Match ID Collision / Overflow
known_vulnerabilities.description: If match ID counter overflows (u64 wrap), IDs could collide, leading to state overwrite or fund theft.
known_vulnerabilities.attack_scenario: 1. MatchCount increments for 2^64 matches; 2. Overflow causes wrap to 0; 3. New match overwrites original match state; 4. Funds stolen or payout replayed
mitigation_implemented: create_match uses: let next_id = id.checked_add(1).ok_or(Error::Overflow)?
checked_add returns None on overflow, triggering Error::Overflow
Contract panics rather than silently wrapping
In practice, u64 space is so large (2^64) that reaching this limit is infeasible
known_vulnerabilities.residual_risk: NEGLIGIBLE - Checked arithmetic + infinite address space

known_vulnerabilities.name: Duplicate Game ID Registration
known_vulnerabilities.description: If game ID uniqueness is not enforced, two matches could claim the same on-chain game, leading to ambiguous oracle results.
known_vulnerabilities.attack_scenario: 1. Player A creates match with game_id='abc123'; 2. Player B creates match with game_id='abc123'; 3. Oracle submits result for 'abc123'; 4. Both matches claim victory?
mitigation_implemented: create_match checks: has(&DataKey::GameId(game_id)) ? Err(DuplicateGameId) : continue
GameId persistent key stored to flag used IDs
Oracle submits to specific match_id (not game_id), so result goes to correct match

known_vulnerabilities.name: Self-Match / Contract Self-Play
known_vulnerabilities.description: If player identity is not validated, a single player could create a match against themselves or the contract, leading to guaranteed wins or fund trapping.
known_vulnerabilities.attack_scenario: 1. Player A creates match(player1=A, player2=A); 2. Player A deposits; 3. Player A wins payout to themselves; 4. Artificial balance inflation
mitigation_implemented: create_match checks: player1 == player2 ? Err(InvalidPlayers) : continue
create_match checks: player2 == contract_address ? Err(InvalidPlayers) : continue
Two distinct non-contract addresses required

known_vulnerabilities.name: Zero or Negative Stake
known_vulnerabilities.description: If stake validation is missing, zero or negative stakes could be accepted, leading to escrow accounting errors.
known_vulnerabilities.attack_scenario: 1. Attacker creates match(stake=-100); 2. Escrow balance becomes negative; 3. Payout tries to transfer -200 (error); 4. Fund trapping
mitigation_implemented: create_match checks: stake_amount <= 0 ? Err(InvalidAmount) : continue
Tier validation also ensures amount > 0 (min stake for each tier > 0)
Only positive stakes accepted

known_vulnerabilities.name: Invalid State Enum Value
known_vulnerabilities.description: If state could take arbitrary values (not enum-constrained), contract behavior becomes undefined.
known_vulnerabilities.attack_scenario: 1. Attacker somehow injects Match.state=999 (invalid); 2. State machine logic fails; 3. Undefined behavior
mitigation_implemented: MatchState is Rust enum with exactly 6 variants: Pending, Active, PendingResult, Completed, Cancelled, Paused
Compiler enforces exhaustive pattern matching
Impossible to have invalid state value at type level
known_vulnerabilities.residual_risk: NEGLIGIBLE - Type system prevents invalid states

known_vulnerabilities.name: Timeout Bounds Violation
known_vulnerabilities.description: If timeout values are not bounded, admin could set excessively short or long timeouts, trapping funds or enabling instant expiration.
known_vulnerabilities.attack_scenario: 1. Admin sets timeout=1 ledger; 2. Pending matches expire instantly; 3. Players unable to deposit; 4. Fund trapping
mitigation_implemented: set_match_timeout checks: timeout < MIN_MATCH_TIMEOUT_LEDGERS || timeout > MAX_MATCH_TIMEOUT_LEDGERS ? Err(InvalidTimeout) : set
Bounded range prevents extreme values

known_vulnerabilities.name: Pause Bypass
known_vulnerabilities.description: If pause mechanism has gaps, critical operations could proceed while contract is paused, violating admin governance.
known_vulnerabilities.attack_scenario: 1. Admin calls pause(); 2. Attacker calls create_match (should be blocked); 3. Match created during pause period
known_vulnerabilities.root_cause: Missing pause checks on sensitive operations
mitigation_implemented: create_match checks: Paused ? Err(ContractPaused) : continue
deposit checks: Paused ? Err(ContractPaused) : continue
submit_result checks: Paused ? Err(ContractPaused) : continue
All mutation operations check pause flag
known_vulnerabilities.residual_risk: LOW - Pause checks cover critical paths

vulnerability_summary.residual_risk: VERY LOW - All identified vulnerabilities have active mitigations

note: Comprehensive vulnerability analysis with all 20 identified risks and their mitigations
